posts.name: Tom Lee
posts.cooked:
Decanted for 30 minutes.

Ruby red in color. 14% ABV. Spellbinding nose of red fruits, Herbes de Provence, garrigue and wet, warm asphalt. Medium body with exquisite acidity. Rustic, old school CdP at its best. Kirsch, blood, licorice and a hint of pepper on the palate. Exceptional length on the finish with pronounced tannins. Fantastic wine with more than enough fruit to keep the tannins in check. If I had more bottles I would drink over the next 2-3 years.

My rating: 95 points.

Full review on Zinfandel Chronicles - 2000 Vieux Télégraphe Châteauneuf-du-Pape – Bottle Notes | Zinfandel Chronicles

Cheers, Tom

posts.name: D Lieberman
posts.cooked: This is pretty much any VT with more then a decade of age on it. These could be my tasting notes for any of the memorable bottles I have had over the years (decades) ; 83, 85, 88, 89, 90. The wine is not flamboyant in its early life, which I suppose is why the critics are usually not so exuberant on release. But given time, it blossoms into something wonderful - perfumed with floral, leathery, beefy tones that are wrapped in a very Burgundian lace - just fantastic. I love that the stuff is relatively under valued in the after market to allow enjoying older bottles at reasonable prices. Just picked up a magnum of the 2000 for under $100! I enjoy the occasional bottle of Pegau, Donjon, Beaucastel,etc., but for my “in the mood for something special from the South”, an older VT is my go to.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Just bought a bottle of this, so great to read.

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
Nice note. VT was one of my gateway wines to Old World back in the day.

You’ve inspired me to open a 95 tonight!

posts.name: Asher Rubinstein
posts.cooked:
I enjoy the occasional bottle of Pegau, Donjon, Beaucastel,etc., but for my “in the mood for something special from the South”, an older VT is my go to.

I know what you mean. I wasn’t drinking as much Chateauneuf anymore, and I sold off a ton of it, but I kept all of my VT. I bought a case of the 1990 a few years ago and every bottle has been great.

posts.name: D Lieberman
posts.cooked:
I enjoy the occasional bottle of Pegau, Donjon, Beaucastel,etc., but for my “in the mood for something special from the South”, an older VT is my go to.

I know what you mean. I wasn’t drinking as much Chateauneuf anymore, and I sold off a ton of it, but I kept all of my VT. I bought a case of the 1990 a few years ago and every bottle has been great.

Asher - I’m so glad that has been your experience also! I still think about the last time I drank an’88. Blissful moment upstairs at Chez Panisse. No other CndP has ever delivered for me as consistently at that level with the exception of Rayas, but I’m just a working stiff, so those older bottles of Rayas will eventually all wind up on the auction block.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
I felt that the 2000 was uncharacteristically austere when it was young and has been blooming recently. I have about seven bottles left from a case.

It’s interesting how some of the different vintages behaved. 1995 was great young and never really shut down, despite some other CdPs like Beaucastel and Pegau having a lot of hardness from that vintage. 1998 was really good young but then spent some time being grumpy. In its early days the 2000 seemed pretty lean compared to those but recent bottles have seemed more in line with what I’d expect from the vintage.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:

Asher - I’m so glad that has been your experience also! I still think about the last time I drank an’88. Blissful moment upstairs at Chez Panisse. No other CndP has ever delivered for me as consistently at that level with the exception of Rayas, but I’m just a working stiff, so those older bottles of Rayas will eventually all wind up on the auction block.

88 was one of the few older VT’s that I found disappointing, at least my one bottle/experience.

It’s amazing how older years can sing, even when not ‘trophy’ types.

No other CndP has ever delivered for me as consistently at that level with the exception of Rayas, but I’m just a working stiff, so those older bottles of Rayas will eventually all wind up on the auction block.

88 was one of the few older VT’s that I found disappointing, at least my one bottle/experience.

It’s amazing how older years can sing, even when not ‘trophy’ types.

Yes Arv! Isn’t that amazing how our experiences can be so individual? One of the things that I find so interesting about VT is that the winemakers seem to pursue a “house style”, that seems more grounded in their own tradition than dependent upon the variables of the vintage itself. My observation is that it makes the VT character more consistent year after year that manifests itself with an almost predictable classic quality after a good decade of cellaring.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
1998 was really good young but then spent some time being grumpy.

I never had a bottle that wasn’t oxidized!

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
William some notes hinting at oxidization. Looks like from reviews there are some really good bottles out there and some less than stellar bottles. I had the wine in 2010 and 2008 and thought it was good.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked: I have one note on the 1998 from my cellar that says “ slightly roasted and a bit mute” but otherwise I think my bottles were good. The most recent was last year at an offline and it was very good. I had at least two from another person’s cellar that were a bit cooked or oxidized but I don’t particularly trust that person’s storage.

posts.name: Damon Levy
posts.cooked: I have a few bottles of VT from vintages around then, but not the 2000. Anyone have recent notes on '96 or '01-'05?

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
I have a few bottles of VT from vintages around then, but not the 2000. Anyone have recent notes on '96 or '01-'05?

I drank a recently purchased bottle of 96 several weeks ago. It was good and there’s no rush to drink, but based on some other experiences with aged VT from less heralded years, I was a little disappointed. Some of the older ones have moved more toward Burgundy structure and Rayas-like flavors, and the one time in the past I had the 96, I thought it might develop that way. This one seemed more like a weaker version of the 95 (which isn’t a bad thing - the 95 is excellent - just not what I was hoping for).

posts.name: Tom Lee
posts.cooked:
1998 was really good young but then spent some time being grumpy.

I never had a bottle that wasn’t oxidized!

Interesting. 98 VT has always been a favorite of mine.

Tom

posts.name: Marshall Manning
posts.cooked: Yeah, I’ve probably tried 8-10 bottles of the '98 VT over the years and have never had an oxidized bottle. I still have a couple left and might check on one this year, but a bottle in 2019 was gorgeous.

posts.name: Al Osterheld
posts.cooked:
I had somewhat mixed experiences with 1998 VT. None were oxidized, but a couple in adolescence were a bit funky and out of sorts. It’s also a bit ripe, to me. One of the distinguishing features of VT is that it does well in some of the not so great years, presumably because it’s in a warm spot. Conversely, it seems a bit less distinguished to me in years that are highly touted (which tend to be the warmer years).

-Al

posts.name: J D o v e

Tom

I went through a lot of it and don’t ever remember any bottles being oxidized. I think I might have one bottle left. I’ll have to try it soon. I will say, I remember that wine with like a dry port when I was first released. It was amazingly dense.
